carrier_description: Anhydrous ammonia (NH3) with typical purity >99.5% for energy applications. Composed of nitrogen and hydrogen. Requires specialized storage and handling infrastructure due to toxicity and reactivity characteristics.

Grid-level electricity transmitted at high voltage (380–220 kV), representing standard transmission infrastructure constraints and voltage level specifications for levels 1–3 of the European electricity grid.
Purity typically exceeds 90% CH₄; infrastructure compatible with natural gas networks and combustion engines.

Grid-level electricity distributed at medium voltage (1–36 kV), typically used for residential and commercial supply. Standard utility resource with no specific purity constraints beyond grid quality specifications.

Thermal Hydrogen typically requires high purity (99.9%+ H2) for efficient use in industrial heating applications. It is transported via dedicated pipelines and stored in pressurized tanks, with infrastructure designed to minimize leakage and ensure safety.
Standard grid-level electricity with typical voltage ranges and infrastructure requirements. No chemical composition constraints as it is an energy carrier.
Thermal energy refers to heat or cooling used in buildings and industrial processes, typically categorised by temperature range and application context.

Heat extracted from the Earth's crust via geothermal energy systems, typically accessed through drilling and heat exchangers. Infrastructure context includes geothermal heat pumps, direct utilization for district heating, and industrial process heat applications. Purity constraints minimal; chemical composition primarily depends on subsurface water/rock interactions.

High-purity hydrogen (typically ≥99.9%) stored and transported under high pressure (70 MPa). Commonly used in fuel cells and industrial processes, requiring specialized infrastructure for handling and distribution.

Pumped hydro storage involves storing energy by moving water between two reservoirs at different elevations. The infrastructure includes pumps, turbines, and reservoirs. Water quality is generally not a constraint as it uses standard water sources.

Hydrogen (H₂) is a colorless, odorless, highly flammable gas with molecular weight 2.016. Purity constraints typically require >99.9% H₂ for most applications, with trace impurities (e.g., CO, CO₂, N₂) depending on production pathway and end-use. Infrastructure requires specialized materials to manage low density, high diffusivity, and flammability characteristics.

Toxic, flammable gas with strong odor; typically present in natural gas and oil deposits. Purity constraints require removal to prevent corrosion and toxicity risks. Infrastructure must include corrosion-resistant materials and safety systems for handling.

Heterogeneous mixture of household and commercial waste, primarily used for energy recovery through incineration or anaerobic digestion. Composition varies by region and includes organic, inorganic, and recyclable materials. Subject to local waste management regulations and quality standards for energy recovery.

Electricity generated via photovoltaic (PV) conversion of solar irradiation, typically using semiconductor-based panels. Infrastructure includes PV modules, inverters, and mounting systems. Purity constraints relate to semiconductor material quality (e.g., silicon purity). No fuel input required.

Solar irradiation available for photovoltaic systems, typically characterised by standard irradiance levels (e.g., 1000 W/m²) and infrastructure context such as rooftop installations or ground-mounted arrays.

Uranium-235 is a fissile isotope of uranium, typically enriched to 3-5% concentration for use in nuclear reactors. It serves as a primary energy source through nuclear fission, requiring stringent purity controls and specialized infrastructure for handling and enrichment. As a naturally occurring, non-renewable resource, its chemical composition is primarily U-235 with trace impurities.
Electricity generated from wind turbines, typically with high purity and integrated into the electricity grid infrastructure.

Solid biomass fuel with typical moisture content below 20%, used in combustion-based heat generation. Composed primarily of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Infrastructure context includes biomass boilers and CHP systems.
High-temperature process heat used in industrial applications, typically requiring temperatures above 200°C for processes such as chemical manufacturing, metallurgy, and high-temperature drying.
Electricity consumed within non-residential buildings, not exported to the grid. Typically sourced from the local distribution network.

Thermal storage refers to the temporary storage of heat energy using materials such as molten salts or phase-change materials. It is not a fuel but a utility for storing thermal energy, often used in conjunction with renewable energy systems or industrial processes.
A heat transfer medium typically used in industrial processes, with standard purity constraints ensuring thermal stability and compatibility with infrastructure.

Carbon 12 refers to the carbon isotope with 6 protons and 6 neutrons, commonly used as a reference standard in various scientific applications. It is typically found in natural carbon sources and may have specific purity requirements depending on its application.

Industrial-grade nitrogen with typical purity constraints of 99.9% or higher, used in inert atmospheres, chemical synthesis, and cryogenic applications. Infrastructure context includes pipeline distribution, cryogenic storage, and on-site generation via air separation units.

Wet biomass characterized by high moisture content (e.g., sewage sludge, food waste), typically requiring preprocessing for energy recovery via anaerobic digestion or thermal treatment. Infrastructure context includes digesters, preprocessing facilities, and waste collection networks.

Typically stored as compressed gaseous CO₂ (99.9% purity) in pipeline infrastructure or geological storage. Infrastructure context includes high-pressure transport and sequestration in saline aquifers/salt caverns.
Standard purity constraints for potable or industrial use, typically H2O with minimal impurities. Infrastructure context includes distribution networks and storage systems.
Typically composed of triglycerides with varying levels of impurities; used as a biofuel or in industrial processes, requiring specific handling and storage infrastructure.
Typically 99.9% pure, used as a fuel and chemical feedstock. Infrastructure includes pipelines, storage tanks, and specialized handling equipment due to its flammability and reactivity.

Carbon monoxide used as a resource within the energy system, typically requiring high purity for industrial applications and integrated into processes such as Fischer-Tropsch synthesis or chemical manufacturing.

Carbon monoxide is typically produced as a component of syngas (CO + H₂) through processes like gasification or reforming. Purity constraints depend on end-use applications (e.g., chemical synthesis, fuel cells), with higher purity required for sensitive processes. Infrastructure context involves handling as a toxic, flammable gas requiring leak-proof containment and ventilation.
Standard octane fuel with typical purity levels for internal combustion engines, composed primarily of hydrocarbons with a high octane rating. Used in gasoline blends for automotive applications.

Oxygen (O₂) is typically supplied at a standard purity of 99.5% or higher, with applications in industrial processes, medical use, and combustion enhancement. It is often produced via air separation units (ASU) or electrolysis of water. Infrastructure considerations include storage in high-pressure cylinders or pipeline distribution systems.

Biochar produced from biomass pyrolysis, typically used for soil amendment or carbon sequestration. High carbon content, low volatility. Infrastructure context includes pyrolysis plants and carbon storage applications.

CO2 Flue Gas typically contains impurities such as nitrogen, oxygen, and particulate matter, with CO2 concentration varying based on the combustion process. It is a waste stream from industrial and power generation facilities.
